content.full_text:
0-76-05-009


May 11, 1976


Mr. R. B. Schmechel
Accountant
COPE
995 Market Street
San Francisco, California  94103

Dear Mr. Schmechel:

This letter is in response to your letter of May 4 requesting
written confirmation of verbal advice concerning reportable
pledges.

You stated that approximately 1500 affiliated locals are required
to pay a "per capita tax" to the California Labor Federation, of
which COPE is a department, each month. You further stated that
approximately 20 percent fail to send in the prescribed amount on
the date that the taxes are due. The only recourse open to the
federation in enforcing the payment of taxes, is to suspend the
local until all back payments are made. The action is a voluntary
payment by the local.

On the basis of the above information, it is our opinion that these
taxes would not be an enforceable promise to make a payment within
the meaning of Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act. The
unpaid per capita taxes of affiliated locals, therefore, are not
considered pledges for campaign reporting purposes. Monies would
be reported as they are received.

Very truly yours,


REGINA SICILIANO-KUTCHINS
Technical Assistance Program
Coordinator

RSK:mak

bcc:  Jeanne
      Nan

sections.conclusion: On the basis of the above information, it is our opinion that these taxes would not be an enforceable promise to make a payment within the meaning of Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act. The unpaid per capita taxes of affiliated locals, therefore, are not considered pledges for campaign reporting purposes. Monies would be reported as they are received.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether per capita taxes owed by affiliated locals to the California Labor Federation constitute reportable pledges under Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that per capita taxes owed to the California Labor Federation are not enforceable promises and therefore do not constitute reportable pledges. Instead, monies should be reported as they are received.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains clear question and answer structure in body text. Letter dated May 11, 1976 responding to May 4 inquiry. Minor OCR issues with signature line formatting.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether per capita taxes owed by affiliated locals to the California Labor Federation constitute reportable pledges under Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: On the basis of the above information, it is our opinion that these taxes would not be an enforceable promise to make a payment within the meaning of Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act. The unpaid per capita taxes of affiliated locals, therefore, are not considered pledges for campaign reporting purposes. Monies would be reported as they are received.

Schmechel Accountant COPE 995 Market Street San Francisco, California 94103 Dear Mr. Schmechel: This letter is in response to your letter of May 4 requesting written confirmation of verbal advice concerning reportable pledges. You stated that approximately 1500 affiliated locals are required to pay a "per capita tax" to the California Labor Federation, of which COPE is a department, each month. You further stated that approximately 20 percent fail to send in the prescribed amount on the date that the taxes are due. The only recourse open to the federation in enforcing the payment of taxes, is to suspend the local until all back payments are made. The action is a voluntary payment by the local. On the basis of the above information, it is our opinion that these taxes would not be an enforceable promise to make a payment within the meaning of Section 82015 of the Political Reform Act. Very truly yours, REGINA SICILIANO-KUTCHINS Technical Assistance Program Coordinator RSK:mak bcc: Jeanne Nan
embedding.summary: This FPPC advice letter responds to COPE's inquiry about whether approximately 20 percent of unpaid per capita taxes from affiliated locals constitute reportable pledges. The FPPC advises that such taxes are voluntary payments without enforcement and therefore are not reportable pledges under the Political Reform Act.
